name: Castro Caldelas – tour around Pobra de Trives – Special Conservation Zone river Navea
name_original: Castro Caldelas – vuelta por Pobra de Trives – Zona Especial de Conservación río Navea
region_intro: Touring cycling around A Pobra De Trives is characterized by its mountainous environment and significant river valleys, offering challenging routes with rewarding panoramic views. The region features dramatic landscapes carved by rivers like the Navea and Bibei, providing varied gradients and scenic backdrops. Cyclists can explore routes that traverse high mountain passes and wind through picturesque canyons. This area, part of the Ourense province in Galicia, presents a blend of physical challenge and natural beauty. Best touring cycling routes around A Pobra De Trives The most popular touring cycling route is Quiroga – tour around high Boi and the rivers Lor and Sil, a 37.0 miles (59.6 km) trail that takes 4 hours 38 minutes to complete. This route features substantial climbs and views over river valleys. Another top favourite among local touring cyclists is Castro Caldelas – tour around Pobra de Trives – Special Conservation Zone river Navea, a difficult 43.5 miles (70.0 km) path. This route traverses the Navea river valley, offering scenic vistas and significant elevation changes. Local touring cyclists also love the A Pobra de Trives Town Center loop from Manzaneda, a 20.9 miles (33.6 km) trail leading through mountainous terrain with views towards Manzaneda, often completed in about 2 hours 28 minutes. Touring cycling around A Pobra De Trives is defined by mountainous terrain, river valleys, and panoramic viewpoints. The network offers options primarily for experienced cyclists seeking challenging ascents and descents. The guide includes highly-rated routes that showcase the best of the region's landscape. More than 90 touring cyclists have used komoot to explore A Pobra De Trives's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: What is the general difficulty level of touring cycling routes around A Pobra de Trives?
A: Touring cycling around A Pobra de Trives is characterized by its challenging mountainous environment. Out of 14 available routes, 12 are rated as difficult, and 2 are moderate. This means most routes feature substantial climbs and descents, making them ideal for experienced cyclists seeking a physical challenge.

Q: Are there any touring cycling routes suitable for less experienced cyclists?
A: While most routes are challenging, there are a couple of moderate options available. These routes still involve significant elevation changes but might be more manageable for those with some cycling experience looking to build up to more difficult terrain. However, there are no easy routes listed in this guide.

Q: What kind of terrain can I expect on touring cycling routes in this region?
A: The terrain around A Pobra de Trives is predominantly mountainous, featuring significant river valleys like the Navea and Bibei. You can expect routes with substantial climbs and rewarding panoramic views, winding through picturesque canyons and high mountain passes. The landscape offers a blend of rugged natural beauty and dramatic scenery.

Q: What historical sites or landmarks can I see along touring cycling routes near A Pobra de Trives?
A: The region is rich in history and offers several points of interest. You can encounter medieval structures like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5014945" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Castro Caldelas Castle&lt;/a&gt;, which offers strategic views of the Sil Canyon. Other notable sites include the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/6560180" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Church of Our Lady of Fátima and Fontei Cross&lt;/a&gt;, and the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/6560182" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Church of San Miguel de Montefurado&lt;/a&gt;. The area also has remnants from the Roman era, such as sections of the ancient Vía Nova.

Q: Are there any scenic viewpoints accessible by touring bike?
A: Yes, the mountainous terrain ensures many routes offer breathtaking vistas. &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/20148345" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;A Pobra de Trives Town Center – Chaira das Lamas Viewpoint loop from O Barrio dos Xitanos&lt;/a&gt; specifically includes a viewpoint. The region is also home to Mirador Pena Folenche, known for its panoramic views of the Terras de Trives and Terras de Caldelas, and the Navea River valley. The nearby Sil Canyon also features impressive viewpoints like Mirador da Pena de Matacás.

Q: Are there any circular touring cycling routes available?
A: Yes, several routes are designed as loops. For instance, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/31877730" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;A Pobra de Trives Town Center loop from Manzaneda&lt;/a&gt; is a 20.9-mile (33.6 km) trail that takes you through mountainous terrain with views towards Manzaneda. Another option is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/38164386" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Castro Caldelas Castle loop from O Burgo&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: What is the best time of year for touring cycling in A Pobra de Trives?
A: Given the mountainous nature of the region, the best time for touring cycling is typically from late spring through early autumn (May to October). During these months, the weather is generally more stable, and temperatures are pleasant for cycling. Winter can bring snow to higher elevations, especially around Cabeza de Manzaneda, making many routes challenging or impassable for touring bikes.

Q: Are touring cycling routes around A Pobra de Trives suitable for cycling with dogs?
A: Due to the challenging terrain, significant elevation changes, and often narrow roads or trails, most touring cycling routes in A Pobra de Trives are not ideally suited for cycling with dogs, especially if they are in a trailer or basket. The focus is on demanding climbs and descents. It's best to consider the specific needs and safety of your dog, and perhaps look for less strenuous, flatter routes if cycling with a pet is a priority.

Q: What are some of the longer touring cycling routes in the area?
A: For those seeking longer rides, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1017320522/castro-caldelas-tour-around-pobra-de-trives-special-conservation-zone-river-navea" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Castro Caldelas – tour around Pobra de Trives – Special Conservation Zone river Navea&lt;/a&gt; is a difficult 43.5-mile (70.0 km) path traversing the Navea river valley. Another substantial option is &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1018822231/quiroga-tour-around-high-boi-and-the-rivers-lor-and-sil" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Quiroga – tour around high Boi and the rivers Lor and Sil&lt;/a&gt;, a 37.0-mile (59.6 km) trail featuring significant climbs and river valley views.

Q: What can I expect from the A Pobra de Trives town center itself?
A: A Pobra de Trives has a charming old town with manor houses and squares, reflecting its historical importance. It offers a pleasant environment for rest and exploration off the bike, providing cultural stops and a glimpse into the local heritage. It can serve as a good starting or ending point for your cycling adventures.

Q: Are there any specific natural reserves or areas of special conservation near the cycling routes?
A: Yes, the region is close to the Reserva da Biosfera Ribeira Sacra e Serras do Oribio e Courel, an area renowned for its natural beauty. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1017320522/castro-caldelas-tour-around-pobra-de-trives-special-conservation-zone-river-navea" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Castro Caldelas – tour around Pobra de Trives – Special Conservation Zone river Navea&lt;/a&gt; route specifically highlights the Navea river valley, which is part of a Special Conservation Zone, offering pristine natural environments and diverse scenery.
tips: A Pobra de Trives is a small regional town located at the foot of the mountain and access to the Manzaneda sky station. It is a great place to stop for lunch whether you go down to the Bibei river or go up to Manzaneda.
Large reservoir that catches the waters of the Queixa river. Located between the Sierra de San Mamede. The construction of the reservoir took 9 years and was completed in 1953.

Magnificent medieval castle located in the town of Castro Caldelas (Ourense). During the Middle Ages it was the most important fortress in the Ribeira Sacra and played a prominent role. The castle was also home to military legions, armies, senior officers and orders of warrior monks.

Trail in the Val de Río Navea National Park, which begins when you turn towards the old Roman bridge over the Navea River and ascend for spectacular views over the surrounding valley landscape. Well worth the detour from the main road.

waypoints.name: A Pobra de Trives Town Center
Protected Landscape of the Navea River Valley
